# Questions in Livestream 49

Live Stream #49: Counting The Omer.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=509" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:29</a> | Is Isaiah 9:6-7 about the future Messiah, the Hezekiah interpretation, or both, and do the verbs imply present or future prophecy? | He says it is both: prophetic poetry can use amphibole to address the Syria-Ephraimite crisis and a future messianic horizon at the same time. | He explains that prophetic poetry often uses amphibole, a literary feature in which one text can refer to multiple things at once. In Isaiah 7-9 he sees the immediate Syria-Ephraimite crisis, Hezekiah's choices, and the future Immanuel or messianic promise all operating together, so he rejects an either-or choice between Hezekiah and the Messiah. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=858" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:18</a> | Is the story about drilling holes in the head to release evil spirits true? | He explains trepanation as a real ancient medical practice, including in Egypt, but says its connection to evil spirits is uncertain. | He identifies the practice as trepanation and says there is evidence for it in ancient Egypt, including skulls where bone began growing back, showing some patients survived. He notes that it may have been used for serious cases such as seizures, but he does not assume the Egyptians necessarily connected those conditions with evil spirits. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=1125" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:45</a> | Is there evidence for Dynasty Zero pharaohs such as Elephant, Crocodile, Scorpion I, and Scorpion II besides Turin Papyrus and pot shards? | Yes. He cites material such as the Scorpion mace head and tombs at Abydos, while noting that much evidence still comes from inscribed pottery. | He says there is evidence beyond the Turin Papyrus, including the Scorpion II mace head and Dynasty Zero royal tombs at Abydos for figures such as Iry-Hor, Ka, and Narmer. He still cautions that numerically much of the evidence comes from pottery sherds with names in Horus serekhs, and he is not sure there were Dynasty Zero pharaohs actually called Elephant or Crocodile. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=1378" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:58</a> | Was the Scorpion King of Semitic origin? | He says probably not; scorpion and snake material is too thin to show Semitic origin, and Egyptian religious imagery is sufficient to explain it. | He says the evidence is not there for a Semitic origin. Some people appeal to Semitic spells against scorpions and snakes, but he calls that thin evidence and says Egyptian animistic and divine imagery already gives enough context for a scorpion-associated king. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=1486" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:46</a> | What are your thoughts on Matthew 1:23's use of Isaiah 7:14, and is Matthew implying additional messianic meaning like dual fulfillment? | He says Matthew draws on the same amphibole already present in Isaiah, with a messianic fulfillment that does not erase the earlier context. | He says Matthew was not alone in reading Isaiah 7:14 messianically, since some Second Temple interpreters did so as well. Matthew uses the broader Hebrew ambiguity of `almah` and the Greek `parthenos`, drawing on the same amphibole in Isaiah so the text can address the earlier sign and also receive messianic fulfillment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=1907" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:47</a> | Was Jeremiah prophesying 70 years under Babylon or 70 years of Babylonian rule after the Assyrians in Jeremiah 25:8-11? | He frames the prophecy around judgment on Judah and exile under Babylon after Babylon inherited former Assyrian territories. | He reads Jeremiah 25 as focused on judgment against Judah and the nations serving Babylon. After Assyria fell, former Assyrian territories were quickly taken over by Babylon, but the burden of the passage is Judah's coming exile under Babylon, with the seventy years connected to that Babylonian domination. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=2224" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:04</a> | Is Deuteronomy 31:24-26 a good precept for Colossians 2:14-16, with covenant laws beside the ark rather than inside it? | He cautions against using Deuteronomy 31 as a direct precept for Colossians 2 because the passages use "law" at different scopes. | He says Deuteronomy 31 is not about the Ten Commandments inside the ark, but about Moses' written book of the law being placed beside the ark as a witness. He distinguishes that wider written Torah from the tablets and notes that other memorial items were placed before or beside the ark, so this should not be flattened into a direct Colossians prooftext. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=2681" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:41</a> | Does Job 39:14-17 accurately describe ostrich behavior, or is it meant only for theological purposes? | He says the passage fits observed ostrich behavior in several ways, especially communal nesting, while still making a theological point from nature. | He argues the passage is biologically more plausible than some skeptical readings admit. Drawing on ostrich communal nesting, he explains that a male, a dominant hen, and minor hens can share one nest, with the male doing most incubation and eggs or young exposed to complex risks, so Job can use real features of ostrich behavior for theological reflection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=3315" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:15</a> | How do you know such niche topics well enough to answer questions like the ostrich question? | He says he reads voraciously, had an undergraduate background in biochemistry, and reads widely beyond his direct specialty. | He says the ostrich material is not his formal specialty, but he reads very widely and retains some background from his undergraduate biochemistry studies. His answer is essentially that broad reading outside Egyptology gives him enough context to engage niche questions cautiously. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=3364" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:04</a> | Did you read Josephus, Tacitus, Eusebius, and Yosippon on armies in the sky, and could this relate to Revelation 19:11-16? | He says he had not yet looked those sources up and therefore passes on the question rather than guessing. | He explicitly says he has not yet been able to look up the cited Josephus, Tacitus, Eusebius, and Yosippon material. Because he has not done that reading, he declines to answer the Revelation connection rather than improvise. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=3442" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:22</a> | A friend said she was a Calvinist; could you explain TULIP and whether it is biblical? | He describes TULIP, says classical Calvinism is within Christian orthodoxy, and warns that extreme forms can become unbiblical. | He defines TULIP as total depravity, unconditional election, limited atonement, irresistible grace, and perseverance of the saints, while noting the acronym's historical framing is debated. He says Calvinism can be an acceptable orthodox Christian position, but the meaning of each point matters and extreme versions can move into unbiblical territory. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=3861" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:21</a> | Does Genesis 15:7-8 contradict Exodus 6:3? | He says no; he leans toward Genesis 15 using a retrojection that links Abraham's God with Yahweh as revealed in Exodus 6. | He discusses the difficulty that Genesis uses Yahweh language before Exodus 6 says God was not known to the patriarchs by that name. He mentions Michael Heiser's question-reading as possible but not fully satisfying, and leans instead toward a narrative retrojection where the writer identifies Abraham's God with Yahweh in light of the later Exodus revelation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=4251" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:51</a> | Was Moses's father-in-law named Reuel, Jethro, or Hobab? | He says the issue is debated, but he prefers the explanation that Moses had more than one wife and therefore more than one father-in-law. | He says all three names are used in the relevant passages, and that interpreters debate whether these are multiple names for one person. He finds that less satisfactory because Numbers 12 shows Moses married a Cushite woman after his earlier marriage, so multiple wives and therefore multiple fathers-in-law better explains the data for him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=4465" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:25</a> | What do you think the "paradise" issue in Luke 23 refers to: Abraham's bosom, heaven, or Eden? | He favors Abraham's bosom as the immediate referent, while allowing that it ultimately connects to heaven after Christ descends and sets captives free. | He says `paradeisos` can mean a pleasant park-like place, but he does not think Luke 23 refers to Eden or immediate heaven. In his explanation, Abraham's bosom is the good compartment of Sheol in Second Temple Jewish cosmology, where the righteous dead awaited Christ's descent and release of the captives. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=4739" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:59</a> | After watching Your Lie in April, do you have any personal anime recommendations? | He recommends several anime or anime films, including Akira, The Wings of Honneamise, Gundam, Black Lagoon, Tenchi Muyo, Death Note, and Tokyo Ghoul. | He says Your Lie in April was excellent but heartbreaking for him, then gives recommendations across different tastes. He names Akira, The Wings of Honneamise, Gundam, Black Lagoon, Tenchi Muyo, and The Girl Who Leapt Through Time, while warning that viewers should check reviews because not all anime is PG-13. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=4967" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:47</a> | Who united Upper and Lower Egypt, and how was it achieved? | He says unification was a process of conquest across the end of Dynasty Zero and into Dynasty One, with Narmer and likely Aha both involved. | He says unification was not achieved by one simple act under one king, but through wars at the end of Dynasty Zero and into Dynasty One. Narmer and Aha both seem to have played roles, with Aha perhaps completing the conquest of Lower Egypt, though he adds that better texts or artifacts could refine that picture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=5135" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:35</a> | Could there be truth to the extrabiblical idea that David and Solomon were exorcists, and does 1 Samuel 16:23 hint at this? | He says the evidence is thin; David's music soothes Saul and the spirit departs, but that is not clearly exorcism. | He reads 1 Samuel 16:23 as David playing music so Saul is refreshed and the evil spirit departs, not as a formal driving out of the spirit. He allows that God can permit an evil spirit within divine sovereignty, but says the passage supports palliative soothing more clearly than exorcism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=5365" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:25</a> | Could you put Leviticus 17:7 into ancient Near Eastern perspective, and what is meant by "devils" there? | He says the word is goat-related and implies a demonic or spiritual entity, with the verse redirecting worship to the tabernacle. | He says the term is goat-related rather than literally "devil," but in Leviticus 17 it represents a demonic or spiritual entity with whom Israel had committed spiritual harlotry. He also connects goat imagery with sin-bearing ideas such as the scapegoat, while stressing that the exact referent is debated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=5649" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:09</a> | Why did Daniel refuse to eat from the king's table in Daniel 1:5-20: kosher concerns or resistance to assimilation? | He says kosher concerns are the main issue, because Daniel was already assimilated into royal service in other ways. | He answers "kosher" directly, explaining that Daniel would not know where royal food came from or whether it included pork, camel, shellfish, or other non-kosher foods. He rejects resistance to assimilation as the main issue because Daniel and his companions were already being incorporated into the royal court in other significant ways. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=5950" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:10</a> | If you were an ancient Egyptian pagan, which god of the Ennead would you worship, and why? | He says probably Seth, partly because of Seth's character and association with pork eating, though Thoth also appeals to him as a scribal god. | He says he would probably pick Seth because Seth is a storm and chaos god, was once a royal deity, and his worshipers were among the Egyptians who could eat pork. He also says Thoth would be a plausible alternative because of writing, magic, and scribal associations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=6102" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:42</a> | How did ancient Egyptians worship their gods, and did average Egyptians have cults? | He says ordinary Egyptians usually worshiped through private shrines, local village shrines, household or ancestral deities, and popular cults such as Hathor and Thoth. | He says average Egyptians generally did not worship inside the major formal temples, which were for kings, priests, and elites. Instead they used household shrines, village shrines, private chapels, ancestor or chosen deities, amulets, and mass-appeal cults such as Hathor and Thoth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=6330" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:30</a> | In Isaiah 13:21, are dancing goats meant to be demons, and is there an ancient Near Eastern way of viewing prophets? | He addresses the goats and says they are best read as literal desert animals in a judgment oracle about Babylon; the prophet question is not clearly answered. | He notes that Isaiah 13:21 uses the same goat-related word that appears in Leviticus 17, but says context matters. In Isaiah's oracle against Babylon, he reads the goats alongside owls, ostriches, and other desert creatures as imagery for an abandoned city rather than as demons; the transcript support for the prophet portion is limited. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=6818" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:38</a> | What do the grammar and morphology of the Hyksos language tell us about the branch of Semitic it most likely came from? | He says the evidence is limited, but names suggest a West Semitic, probably Amorite-influenced, background. | He cautions that there is not enough Hyksos material to reconstruct a grammar: mostly names, scarabs, and a few small texts. What can be seen includes Semitic names and Amorite imperfective name patterns, so he places the language in a West Semitic sphere with Amorite influence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=6993" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:33</a> | What are the weirdest Egyptian texts you have read? | He names Papyrus Westcar, especially the magician story with the wax crocodile. | He names Papyrus Westcar as the strangest, especially the story where a magician deals with an unfaithful wife by using a wax crocodile and walking backward to the river. He says the scene feels alien and odd when first reading Egyptian texts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=7139" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:59</a> | What are your thoughts on David's census in 2 Samuel 24:1 and 1 Chronicles 21:1? | He says Israel's kings were not supposed to take censuses because they encouraged pride and enabled taxation or corvee labor. | He distinguishes David's census from the tribal or clan censuses in Numbers, which he says displayed God's preservation of family units. David's census was a royal count of subjects, which he says Israel's kings were not supposed to take because it encouraged pride and could enable taxation or forced labor, later associated with Solomon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=7545" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:45</a> | Does seeming livelier tonight mean you have recovered from COVID? | He says he has mostly recovered, though he still has sinus drip, coughing, bronchitis, and some lightheadedness. | He says he is mostly recovered but still has sinus drip, coughing, bronchitis, and some lightheadedness. He explains respiratory illnesses are serious for him because of a compromised respiratory system, though this COVID bout was not as bad as he had feared or as severe as a previous whooping cough episode. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=7659" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:39</a> | Was Melchizedek Shem or a Jebusite? | He says Melchizedek was not Shem and is better understood as a Jebusite ruler-priest. | He rejects the rabbinic identification of Melchizedek with Shem, partly because he prefers the Septuagint lifespan readings over the Masoretic chronology behind that argument. He says Jerusalem was originally a Jebusite city, and Melchizedek's name is a Jebusite theophoric name connected with the god Zadok, so he sees him as a Jebusite priest-king. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=7833" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:33</a> | Why would Yahweh make himself known as El Shaddai when there was already a Ugaritic El? | He says "El" can function as both a proper name and a common Semitic word for god, so names like El Shaddai specify which god is meant. | He explains that `El` can be both the proper name of the high god in the Ugaritic pantheon and a common Semitic noun meaning god. Because that root is widespread across Semitic languages, titles such as El Shaddai or El Elyon identify which god is meant without requiring Yahweh to be the same as Ugaritic El. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=8017" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:37</a> | What are your thoughts on Hugh Ross's argument that Psalm 104 is a creation narrative and that verses 6-9 preclude a global flood reading? | He says Psalm 104 is a creation narrative, but he does not think the passage clearly precludes or proves a global flood reading. | He says Psalm 104 has creation material and is adapted from an Egyptian hymn, but he is not convinced verses 6-9 settle the flood question. The waters fleeing and boundaries being set can be read as creation imagery, yet flood readers can also interpret the mountains rising and waters receding in flood terms, so he says the passage neither proves nor precludes a global flood. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=8507" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:47</a> | Are you a fan of the Legend of Drizzt series? | He says he has not read the Drizzt books, though his wife likes them, and he has read only a few Dungeons & Dragons novels. | He says he has not read the Drizzt or Dark Elf series, although his wife says they are her favorite. He has read some science fiction, fantasy, and a few Dungeons & Dragons novels, but he was more of a role-playing game player than a reader of that novel line. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=8610" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:30</a> | What type of YouTube videos or podcasts do you watch at work? | He mentions Ukrainian language lessons, finance and cryptocurrency material, paranormal-experience podcasts, debates, and sometimes Jimmy Akin's channel. | He says he often listens to Ukrainian language lessons because he has Ukrainian friends, as well as finance and cryptocurrency podcasts. If time remains, he listens to paranormal-experience material from Christian, non-Christian, and skeptical angles, along with debates and sometimes Jimmy Akin's channel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=8870" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:50</a> | May I include your answer on the Hugh Ross question in my references for an upcoming video? | He says yes, including an appropriate clip, but asks to be told the video and channel so he can recognize the permitted use. | He gives permission to include the answer and even an appropriate clip, but asks the viewer to tell him the video and channel. His reason is practical: YouTube may flag copied video content, and he wants to distinguish permitted use from cases where people reposted material without credit or endorsement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=9107" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:47</a> | Do you think the Heavenly Book of the Cow usurped the divine epithet Yahweh, namely "I am that I am"? | He says no; he thinks the Egyptian phrase is not "I am that I am" and criticizes the argument as linguistically weak. | He says he does not think the Heavenly Book of the Cow uses the Yahweh epithet; at most, he recalls something closer to "the one who is," not "I am that I am." He adds that the proposed syntax would be very strange in Egyptian and criticizes John Currid's Egyptological and linguistic handling as weak. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9yvb8Yl3hao?t=9791" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:11</a> | Other than Kitchen, who is your favorite scholar? | He names Lorenzo Nigro and Brent Strawn as younger scholars whose work he appreciates. | He narrows the question to newer scholars rather than naming a single overall favorite. He praises Lorenzo Nigro's excavation work at Jericho and Brent Strawn's Old Testament scholarship, especially The Old Testament Is Dying, calling Strawn one of the promising futures of responsible Christian scholarship. |
